title: Cat White Rice-Like Segments on Anus: Identifying Tapeworms
meta_desc: Seeing rice-like segments on your cat's anus? Learn why this usually indicates tapeworms, the level of urgency, and how to get the right treatment.
content_html:
What Are Those White Segments?

If you notice small, white, or cream-colored segments that look like grains of rice around your cat's anus or in their bedding, you are likely seeing tapeworm proglottids. These are individual segments of a tapeworm that have broken off and are filled with eggs. They may move or wiggle slightly when fresh, but will eventually dry out and look like hard yellow sesame seeds.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

The urgency level for tapeworms is considered low. While the sight of worms can be distressing, tapeworms are rarely a life-threatening emergency for otherwise healthy adult cats. However, they can cause discomfort, itching, and mild weight loss over time. You should schedule a routine appointment with your veterinarian within the next few days to obtain the correct prescription deworming medication.

Why a Photo Helps Your Vet

When you call or visit your vet, providing a clear, close-up photo of the segments can significantly speed up the triage process. Different types of parasites require different medications; seeing the physical characteristics of the segments allows the vet to confirm the species visually. This ensures your cat receives the most effective treatment without the need for unnecessary testing.

faqs.question: How did my cat get tapeworms?
faqs.answer: The most common way cats contract tapeworms is by ingesting an infected flea while grooming themselves or by eating infected prey like mice.

faqs.question: Can humans get tapeworms from cats?
faqs.answer: It is very rare. A human would have to accidentally ingest an infected flea to contract the same type of tapeworm found in cats. Standard hygiene like handwashing is effective prevention.

faqs.question: Do I need to treat for fleas as well?
faqs.answer: Yes. Because fleas are the primary carrier of tapeworms, the infection will likely return if you do not implement a strict flea prevention plan alongside the deworming medication.
